revision.text:
[[ja:郊祀]] '''Kōshi''' (郊祀) Chinese: jiaoji also known as suburban worship refers to a ritual in {{ill|China|en|China|qid=Q148|lt=China|1=China}} where the {{ill|Son of Heaven|en|Son of Heaven|qid=Q1147432|lt=Emperor|1=Son of Heaven}} conducted worship of Heaven and Earth (天地) in the suburbs of the royal capital.https://books.google.com/books/about/General_History_of_Religions_in_China.html?id=0gICmwEACAAJ&source=kp_book_description age 107http://www.chinaknowledge.de/History/Terms/jiaosi.htmlhttps://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/10971467.2020.1800905https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/abs/10.1080/10971467.2020.1800905https://religio.amursu.ru/index.php/en/new-archive/92-articles-en/3-2022/1262-on-ritual-sacrifices-to-heaven-jiao-in-jin-dynasty-based-on-the-materials-of-28th-juan-jin-shihttps://www.degruyter.com/document/doi/10.1515/9780824894344-033/pdf?licenseType=restricted&srsltid=AfmBOooEWd2h5Ud8IksVh3vYM9ip5aS1oc83d8AobDgnlhsBTI3IHM7i The ritual involved building a circular mound and included worship of {{ill|ancestors of the Emperor|ja|皇祖神|1=Ancestors of the Emperor|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ancestors of the Emperor|13=User:Immanuelle/Ancestors of the Emperor|qq=}}, hence also called the "Circular Mound Rite" (円丘祭). During the {{ill|Winter solstice|en|Winter solstice|qid=Q207932|lt=Winter solstice|1=Winter solstice}}, the Son of Heaven personally traveled to the southern suburbs of the capital to worship {{ill|Heaven|en|Heaven|qid=Q4489450|lt=Heaven|1=Heaven}}, and during the {{ill|Summer solstice|en|Summer solstice|qid=Q7603898|lt=Summer solstice|1=Summer solstice}}, he traveled to the northern suburbs to worship the Earth. Detailed descriptions of these rituals are preserved in texts such as {{ill|The Kaiyuan Rituals of the Tang Dynasty|zh|大唐开元礼|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/The Kaiyuan Rituals of the Tang Dynasty|qq=}}. Lesser versions were held in the {{ill|Jiao_(commercial_guild)|en|Jiao_(commercial_guild)|qid=Q10556778|lt=Jiao (suburbs)|1=Jiao (commercial guild)}} of various cities https://www2.kenyon.edu/Depts/Religion/Fac/Adler/Reln270/jiaorite.htm They were often done in different suburbs corresponding to the {{ill|Wufang Shangdi|en|Wufang Shangdi|qid=Q9384963|lt=Wufang Shangdi|1=Wufang Shangdi}}.https://books.google.com/books/about/General_History_of_Religions_in_China.html?id=0gICmwEACAAJ&source=kp_book_descriptionChapter 5 == Kōshi in Japan == [[File:Sugigamoto-jinja, entrance.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Sugigamoto Shrine, the presumed location
({{ill|Hirakata|en|Hirakata|qid=Q270912|lt=Hirakata|1=Hirakata}} Katahoko Honmachi)}}]] [[File:Katanoamatsukami-no-yashiro, sekihi.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Monument commemorating Emperor Kanmu
({{ill|Katano Tenjin Shrine|ja|交野天神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katano Tenjin Shrine|qq=}})
({{ill|Hirakata|en|Hirakata|qid=Q270912|lt=Hirakata|1=Hirakata}} Kuzuhaoka)}}]] In Japan, the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|qid=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}} describes {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|qid=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu|1=Emperor Jimmu}} performing a kōshi ritual at {{ill|Mount Tori|ja|鳥見山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Tori|1=Mount Torimi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q18700112}} after his enthronement by establishing a sacred site called a "reijin" (霊畤). However, this account is considered to be a later embellishment, and the content of the described ritual differs from the Chinese kōshi. The first kōshi influenced by Chinese practices occurred under {{ill|Emperor Kanmu|en|Emperor Kanmu|qid=Q314846|lt=Emperor Kanmu|1=Emperor Kanmu}} as part of his Sinicization policies. It was conducted on {{ill|November 10, 785|ja|延暦4th year 11月10日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/November 10, 785|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|November 5, 787|ja|延暦6th year 11月5日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/November 5, 787|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Kashiwabara of Katano District in {{ill|Kawachi Province|ja|河内国交野郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawachi Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (present-day Katahoko, {{ill|Hirakata|en|Hirakata|qid=Q270912|lt=Hirakata|1=Hirakata}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|qid=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture|1=Osaka Prefecture}}). A small circular mound that existed in this area until the early Showa period is believed to be the site of this ritual.{{ill|Rikurō Hayashi|ja|林陸朗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rikurō Hayashi|1=Rokurō Hayashi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q30604846}}, "Kōshi Circular Mound in Katano" (『平安時代史事典』 Kadokawa Shoten, 1994, ISBN 978-4-04-031700-7) p. 496. During the second ceremony, Emperor Kanmu also worshipped his father, {{ill|Emperor Kōnin|en|Emperor Kōnin|qid=Q958662|lt=Emperor Kōnin|1=Emperor Kōnin}}, and adopted the Chinese format for ritual texts, addressing the {{ill|Jade Emperor|en|Jade Emperor|qid=Q860434|lt=Jade Emperor|1=Jade Emperor}}.This is the earliest recorded appearance of a "ritual text" (祭文) in Japanese historical records.{{Sfn|Murayama|1979|p=43}}. This practice aimed to rebuild the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令政治|Ritsuryō=Ritsuryō|lt=ritsuryō polity|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ritsuryō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 律令制}} by incorporating Chinese cultural elements. Additionally, it symbolized the establishment of a "new dynasty" after the restoration of the {{ill|Emperor Tenji|en|Emperor Tenji|qid=Q314802|lt=Emperor Tenji|1=Emperor Tenji}} lineage, which had been interrupted since the {{ill|Jinshin War|en|Jinshin War|qid=Q2378252|lt=Jinshin War|1=Jinshin War}}. Later, during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Montoku|en|Emperor Montoku|qid=Q357225|lt=Emperor Montoku|1=Emperor Montoku}}, another kōshi was conducted on {{ill|November 22, 856|ja|斉衡3rd year 11月22日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/November 22, 856|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and the following day, again in Kashiwabara (recorded in the {{ill|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|en|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|qid=Q1052905|lt=Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|1=Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku}}). However, by this time, the capital had long been moved to {{ill|Heian-kyō|en|Heian-kyō|qid=Q751907|lt=Heian-kyō|1=Heian-kyō}}, making Kashiwabara geographically distant. It remains unclear why a closer site was not chosen, or why kōshi, after a gap of more than 70 years, was revived. Moreover, only three instances of kōshi are documented in Japanese history: the two in Enryaku and one in Jōwa. The reasons behind these occurrences remain enigmatic. == Notes == === Explanatory Notes === {{reflist|group=notes}} === References === {{reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|author=Shūichi Murayama|authorlink=村山修一|chapter=Ritual Texts|editor=Editorial Committee for the Japanese Historical Dictionary|year=1979|month=November|title=Japanese Historical Dictionary, Volume 5: Sa to Shi|publisher={{ill|Kawade Shobō Shinsha|en|Kawade Shobō Shinsha|qid=Q11141263|lt=Kawade Shobō Shinsha|1=Kawade Shobō Shinsha}}|isbn=|ref=Murayama}} * {{Cite book|author=Kōshō Mori|chapter=Kōshi|editor=Editorial Committee for the Great Japanese History Dictionary|year=1992|title=Great Japanese History Dictionary, Volume 1|publisher={{ill|Heibonsha|en|Heibonsha|qid=Q11482108|lt=Heibonsha|1=Heibonsha}}|isbn=978-4-582-13101-7}} == See Also == * {{ill|Sacrifice to Heaven|en|Sacrifice to Heaven|qid=Q11591092|lt=Sacrifice to Heaven|1=Sacrifice to Heaven}} * {{ill|Chinese Imperial Rituals|ja|皇帝祭祀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinese Imperial Rituals|qq=}} == External Links == * [https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%83%8A%E7%A5%80-62138#E3.83.96.E3.83.AA.E3.82.BB%E3.83.8B%E3.82.AB%E5.9B.BD%E9.9A.9B%E5.A4.A7.E7.99.BE%E7.A7.91%E4.BA.8B.E5.85.B8 郊祀とは] - {{ill|Kotobank|en|Kotobank|qid=Q11302260|lt=Kotobank|1=Kotobank}} * [https://www.weblio.jp/content/%E9%83%8A%E7%A5%80 郊祀とは] - {{ill|Weblio|en|Weblio|qid=Q11254147|lt=Weblio|1=Weblio}} Dictionary of World Religions {{translated page|ja|郊祀|version=102283022|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:郊祀]] {{wikidata link|Q11643067}} [[Category:Chinese culture]] [[Category:Festivals]] [[Category:Religious Confucianism]] [[Category:Rituals]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]
